_metadata.description: Comprehensive knowledge base for residential construction stages. Used by AI extraction and generation modules.

stages.name: Foundation
stages.description: Creates the structural base that transfers all building loads safely to stable soil or bedrock. The foundation determines the structural integrity and longevity of the entire building. Errors at this stage cannot be corrected later without demolishing and rebuilding.

inspection_points.timing: After rebar set, before concrete pour

common_delays.description: Heavy rain flooding excavation or compromising freshly poured concrete
Unexpected rock, soft soil, or high water table requiring additional engineering
Building permit not yet issued before excavation can begin
Ready-mix truck delays causing cold joints or compromised pour
Underground utilities discovered during excavation requiring rerouting
Inspector not available within required timeframe

safety_hazards.description: Cave-in of excavation walls. OSHA's #1 cause of construction fatalities. Any excavation over 5 feet deep requires shoring, sloping, or shielding.
prevention: Never enter unprotected trench over 5 feet
Keep heavy equipment away from trench edges

safety_hazards.description: Wet concrete has pH 12-13 (same as bleach). Prolonged skin or eye contact causes severe chemical burns.
prevention: Wear rubber boots, gloves, long sleeves
Never kneel in wet concrete without knee protection

safety_hazards.description: Workers struck by excavator bucket, concrete truck, or other heavy equipment.
prevention: Maintain safe distance from operating equipment

safety_hazards.description: Excavator hitting buried electrical conduit.
prevention: Hand dig within 18 inches of marked utilities

stages.weather_notes: Cannot pour concrete in temperatures below 40°F (4°C) without cold-weather protection. Cannot pour during rain. High winds can dry concrete too quickly.

stages.name: Concrete Flatwork
stages.description: Placement and finishing of horizontal concrete surfaces including garage slabs, driveways, walkways, patios, and floor slabs. Requires precision finishing to achieve the specified slope, texture, and surface quality.

curing_period.notes: Concrete reaches 70% strength at 7 days, full design strength at 28 days

common_delays.description: Rain during or immediately after pour ruins surface finish
Temperatures above 90°F (32°C) cause rapid evaporation and cracking
Below 50°F (10°C) requires heated enclosures or blankets
Delayed trucks create cold joints in the pour
Concrete pump failure during pour

safety_hazards.description: Same as foundation stage. Particularly dangerous during finishing when workers kneel in wet mix.
Repetitive motion and kneeling during finishing causes chronic back and knee injuries.
Finishers working in direct sun on reflective concrete surface in summer.

stages.weather_notes: Weather is the primary risk for this stage. Rain on fresh concrete ruins the surface finish. Temperature extremes require special precautions.

stages.name: Framing
stages.description: Construction of the structural skeleton of the building including floor systems, wall assemblies, and roof structure. Framing defines all room dimensions, ceiling heights, and structural load paths. This is the fastest-changing and most visually dramatic stage of construction.

workers.notes: Only when setting roof trusses or heavy beams

materials.name: LVL beam (Laminated Veneer Lumber)
materials.notes: Lead time 2-4 weeks from engineered lumber supplier

materials.name: Roof trusses
materials.notes: Factory fabricated, must be ordered 3-6 weeks in advance

key_milestones: First floor walls framed and plumb

inspection_points.timing: After all rough-in framing complete, before any covering

common_delays.description: Dimensional lumber delivery delays from supply chain issues
LVL beams and engineered lumber have 2-4 week lead times
Roof trusses are custom-made and require 3-6 week lead times
Cannot work on elevated framing in high winds (OSHA: stop at 25+ mph for elevated work)
Owner or architect changes window/door locations mid-framing
Crane not available on day trusses arrive

safety_hazards.description: Falls are the #1 cause of construction fatalities. Framers work at heights from 6 to 30+ feet without fall protection when working on top of walls or roof structure.
prevention: Safety harnesses and lanyards on roof
Guard rails on open floor edges
Never throw tools or materials from height

safety_hazards.description: Framing nail guns fire 3.5" nails at high speed. Contact trip nailers (most common type) can double-fire unexpectedly. 37,000 nail gun injuries annually in USA.
prevention: Use sequential trip nailers when possible
Never carry with finger on trigger

safety_hazards.description: Long lumber pieces falling or whipping when cut. Wall panels tipping during erection.
prevention: Clear area below before cutting or raising material
Wear hard hats at all times

stages.weather_notes: Rain slows work but does not stop it for framing. High winds above 25 mph stop elevated work. Lumber must be protected from prolonged rain exposure.

stages.name: Roofing
stages.description: Installation of the complete roofing system to make the structure weathertight. The roof is a critical milestone: once complete, interior work can proceed in any weather. Includes underlayment, shingles or other finish material, flashing at all penetrations, and ventilation.

materials.name: Ice and water shield
materials.notes: Required in valleys and at eaves in cold climates

key_milestones: Ice and water shield installed at eaves and valleys
Drip edge installed at rakes (over underlayment)
Step flashing installed at all walls

inspection_points.timing: Some jurisdictions inspect roofing, others rely on permit holder certification

common_delays.description: Cannot apply shingles during rain. Wet decking must dry before shingling.
OSHA requires stopping roofing in high winds. Risk of shingles blowing off before nailed.
Shingles are heavy (80 lbs per bundle, 3 bundles per square). Delivery scheduling.
Damaged or rotten roof decking discovered during tearoff or new construction.

safety_hazards.description: Roofers have the highest fall fatality rate of any construction trade. Falls from residential roofs are typically 15-30 feet.
prevention: Personal fall arrest system (harness) required above 6 feet
Roof jacks and planks for steep pitches
Never work on wet or icy roof

safety_hazards.description: Roof surface temperatures can be 50-70°F above ambient air temperature in summer. Working on a black asphalt shingle roof in August can be in 150°F+ surface conditions.
prevention: Start work at sunrise, stop in peak heat
Minimum 1 quart water per hour per worker

safety_hazards.description: Roofing nail guns with bump trigger are particularly dangerous on steep surfaces.
prevention: Maintain three points of contact before firing
Never let go of gun while firing
Keep others below away from nail trajectory

stages.weather_notes: Most weather-sensitive stage after flatwork. Rain stops all work. Wind above 30 mph stops work. Morning dew must dry before starting.

stages.name: Electrical Rough-In
stages.description: Installation of all electrical infrastructure inside the walls, floors, and ceiling before drywall is installed. Includes the main panel, all circuit wiring, outlet boxes, switch boxes, and fixture boxes. This work must pass inspection before drywall covers it permanently.

workers.notes: Must be licensed in the state

materials.name: Conduit EMT 1/2" and 3/4"
materials.notes: For exposed runs and service entrance

materials.name: Arc fault circuit interrupters (AFCI)
materials.notes: Required by NEC 2020 for most circuits

tools_and_equipment: Drill with long bit (spade bits, ship auger)
key_milestones: All boxes installed at correct heights

inspection_points.timing: After all wiring complete, before insulation or drywall

common_delays.description: Electrical inspection must be scheduled 24-72 hours in advance
Specific panel models (especially with shortages) on backorder
Specific circuit breaker models hard to source
Must coordinate drill holes with framers, plumbers, HVAC to avoid conflicts in walls
Owner changes outlet or fixture locations requiring re-wiring

safety_hazards.description: Working near energized conductors during service connection. Temporary power on site can be live.
prevention: Verify de-energized with tester before touching
Only master electrician works on service entrance
GFCI protection on all temporary power

safety_hazards.description: Electrical arc from panel or service can cause severe burns and blast trauma.
prevention: Arc-rated PPE when working near energized panels
Never work in panel with power on unless required

safety_hazards.description: Working from ladders in attic, crawl space, or elevated positions.

stages.weather_notes: Electrical rough-in is mostly interior work after roofing. Can continue in all weather once roof is on.

stages.name: HVAC Rough-In
stages.description: Installation of heating, ventilation, and air conditioning ductwork and refrigerant lines inside walls, floors, and ceilings before drywall. Includes all duct runs, registers, air handler platform, and refrigerant line sets. Must be designed to ACCA Manual D standards for proper airflow.

materials.name: Refrigerant line set (copper)
materials.notes: Pre-insulated copper tubing for AC refrigerant

tools_and_equipment: Sheet metal snips (left, right, straight cut)
key_milestones: All branch ducts run to registers

inspection_points.timing: After all duct runs complete, before insulation or drywall

common_delays.description: HVAC equipment (air handler, condenser) can have 4-16 week lead times during shortages
Duct routes conflict with structural members, electrical, or plumbing
Load calculation revision changes duct sizing
HVAC inspection scheduling

safety_hazards.description: Sheet metal edges are razor-sharp. Most HVAC injuries are lacerations to hands and forearms.
prevention: Heavy leather gloves when handling duct
Never grab sheet metal edges bare-handed

safety_hazards.description: Refrigerants are stored under pressure. Release can cause cryogenic burns and asphyxiation in enclosed spaces.
HVAC rough-in often requires extended work in attic spaces that reach 140°F+ in summer.
prevention: Work in attic only in morning
Exit attic immediately if symptoms appear

stages.weather_notes: Interior work after roofing. Not weather-dependent.

stages.name: Plumbing Rough-In
stages.description: Installation of all drain-waste-vent (DWV) piping and water supply lines inside walls, floors, and ceilings before drywall. Includes all drain lines from fixtures to sewer, all vent stacks through roof, and hot/cold supply lines to every fixture location. Must pass pressure test before being covered.
key_milestones: Under-slab drains complete (if applicable, before concrete pour)
Main drain line to sewer complete
All vent stacks to roof complete
All vent penetrations through roof flashed
Water supply lines run to all fixture locations

inspection_points.timing: Before slab concrete pour - critical milestone
After all rough-in complete, pressure test witnessed, before drywall

common_delays.description: Under-slab drain layout conflicts with structural foundation design
Plumbing inspection requires 24-72 hour advance scheduling
Air or water pressure test fails due to leak in DWV or supply system
Custom fixtures (freestanding tubs, specialty items) have 6-12 week lead times

safety_hazards.description: PVC primer and cement are highly flammable and cause burns. Solvents have toxic vapors.
prevention: Proper storage - away from heat sources

safety_hazards.description: Propane/MAPP torch used for copper soldering. Fire risk from torch near wood framing.
prevention: Heat shield behind copper while soldering near wood
Inspect for smoldering after torch work
Wait 30 minutes after torch work in area before leaving

safety_hazards.description: Heavy pipe handling, crawl space work, and overhead work in confined areas.
prevention: Team lift for heavy pipe runs

stages.weather_notes: Interior work after roofing. Not weather-dependent except that PVC cement requires temperature above 40°F to cure properly.

stages.name: Drywall (Gypsum Board)
stages.description: Installation of gypsum board panels on all interior wall and ceiling surfaces. The process has three sequential phases: hanging (attaching panels), taping (embedding paper tape in joints), and finishing (applying multiple coats of joint compound). Each phase requires the previous to be complete and dry. This stage closes all rough-in work permanently.

taping_and_finishing_2000sqft.notes: Each coat must dry 24 hours minimum before next coat

tools_and_equipment: Spray texture gun (for knockdown or orange peel)
key_milestones: Tape coat (first coat) applied and dry

inspection_points.timing: Must pass before drywall covers insulation
Some jurisdictions inspect drywall, particularly for fire-rated assemblies

common_delays.description: Cannot start drywall until all rough-in inspections (electrical, plumbing, HVAC) have passed
Each coat of joint compound must dry completely - humidity delays drying significantly
High humidity prevents joint compound from drying, causing it to crack or mold
Owner undecided on texture type causes delay

safety_hazards.description: Drywall compound contains crystalline silica. Sanding creates very fine respirable dust. Chronic exposure causes silicosis (incurable, fatal lung disease).
prevention: N95 or P100 respirator during all sanding
Ventilate space during and after sanding

safety_hazards.description: 4x12 drywall sheets weigh 77 lbs. Ceiling hanging requires lifting overhead. Back and shoulder injuries are common.
prevention: Always use drywall lift for ceilings

safety_hazards.description: Drywall finishers use stilts to reach ceilings. Falls from stilts cause ankle fractures and head injuries.
prevention: Hard hat when on stilts near other workers above

stages.weather_notes: Humidity is the main concern. Cannot achieve good finish in high humidity. Space may need dehumidification in wet climates.

stages.name: Painting
stages.description: Application of primer and finish paint coats to all interior walls, ceilings, and trim, plus exterior surfaces. Painting is both protective (moisture barrier, UV protection) and aesthetic. Quality of drywall prep directly determines paint quality. Proper sequencing with other finish trades is critical to avoid damage.
key_milestones: Primer coat inspected and touch-ups done

common_delays.description: Poor drywall sanding or priming requires additional prep work before paint can proceed
Cannot paint below 50°F or above 95°F or above 85% humidity
Owner changes paint colors after first coat, requiring additional coats
Other trades damage painted surfaces requiring touch-up or repainting
Painting must coordinate with trim - prime before trim is hung, finish after trim in place

safety_hazards.description: Paint fumes (particularly oil-based paints, primers, and specialty coatings) contain volatile organic compounds that cause respiratory harm and are flammable.
prevention: Use low-VOC or zero-VOC paints where possible
Adequate ventilation during and after application
Organic vapor respirator for oil-based or spray work

safety_hazards.description: Painters use ladders and extension poles. Reaching too far while on ladder is a common fall cause.
Airless sprayers operate at 2000-3000 PSI. Contact with skin causes paint injection under skin requiring emergency surgery.
prevention: Never point sprayer at body parts
Trigger guard on when not spraying
Immediate emergency medical care for any injection injury

stages.weather_notes: Exterior painting highly weather-dependent. Interior painting only affected by humidity and temperature extremes.

stages.name: Finishing
stages.description: All interior finish work that completes the building's livable condition. This stage encompasses multiple sub-trades running in sequence and sometimes parallel: flooring, finish carpentry (trim, molding), cabinet installation, countertops, door hanging, and completion of electrical, plumbing, and HVAC finish items. Sequencing between sub-trades is critical to avoid damage.

typical_duration.notes: Duration depends heavily on material selection, custom orders, and trade sequencing

key_milestones: Tile complete and grouted in all wet areas
Base molding and door casing installed
Interior doors hung and hardware installed

common_delays.description: Custom cabinets 6-12 weeks, stone countertops 2-4 weeks, custom tile 4-8 weeks
Each sub-trade must complete before next can start in same room
Owners undecided on flooring type, cabinet style, or hardware delays procurement
Flooring damaged by later trade. Painted surfaces chipped. Sequential trades cause rework.
Countertops must be templated after cabinets are installed and level - adds 1-2 weeks

safety_hazards.description: Miter saws, table saws, and tile saws cause the most finish trade injuries. Lacerations and amputations.
prevention: Diamond blade for tile (no toothed), wet saw only

safety_hazards.description: Dry cutting ceramic or porcelain tile creates very high silica dust concentrations.
prevention: N95 minimum respirator if any dust present

safety_hazards.description: Flooring adhesives, grout sealers, and caulks emit harmful vapors particularly in enclosed spaces.
prevention: Ventilate all spaces during and after adhesive work

stages.weather_notes: Mostly interior work. Humidity can affect wood flooring installation (wood expands and contracts).

stages.name: Inspection
stages.description: Official inspections by the Authority Having Jurisdiction (AHJ) - typically the local building department. Inspections occur at multiple stages throughout construction, not just at the end. Each inspection verifies compliance with local building codes, state codes, and adopted model codes (IRC, NEC, etc.). A failed inspection requires correction and re-inspection before work can proceed.

if_failed_requires_correction.notes: Time to correct deficiencies and reschedule

workers.notes: Must be present for all inspections
Trade subcontractor may need to be present for their inspection

tools_and_equipment: Building plans (approved permit set - must be on site)
Inspection card (must be posted on site)
key_milestones: Pre-pour foundation - before any concrete placed
Framing - after all rough-in trades complete
Rough plumbing - pressure test witnessed, before drywall

inspection_points.timing: Final inspection passed = CO issued = building legally occupiable

common_delays.description: Inspections typically require 24-72 hour advance notice. Some jurisdictions have 1-2 week wait times.
Failing any inspection requires correction and rescheduling, adding 2-7 days minimum
Inspector will not conduct inspection without approved permit drawings on site
Inspector requires responsible party present. If no one is there, inspection is cancelled.
Inspector interprets code differently than contractor, requiring plan check or variance

safety_hazards.description: Inspectors enter construction sites that may have incomplete conditions (open holes, temporary power, etc.).
prevention: Ensure site is safe before inspection
Hard hats and safety vest for all on site
Notify inspector of any specific site hazards

construction_sequencing_rules.description: Rules that govern the valid sequence of construction stages. Used by dataset generators and AI validation to ensure realistic logs.
hard_rules: foundation must complete before framing begins
framing must complete before roofing begins
framing must complete before electrical_rough_in begins
framing must complete before hvac_rough_in begins
framing must complete before plumbing_rough_in begins
roofing should be substantially complete before interior finish work begins
electrical_rough_in must pass inspection before insulation begins
plumbing_rough_in must pass inspection before insulation begins
hvac_rough_in must pass inspection before insulation begins
insulation must pass inspection before drywall begins
drywall must complete before painting begins
painting prime coat should happen before cabinets are installed
painting finish coat should happen before final flooring in most rooms
tile work can happen parallel to painting in different areas
under_slab_plumbing must complete before concrete_flatwork in garage or interior slabs
